For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Both clicks worked:
1. Click (32, 14) - 2 cells up-left from square at (33-34, 15-16) → moved to (31-32, 13-14)
2. Click (32, 15) - 1 cell above square at (31-32, 13-14) → moved to (31-32, 14-15)
So the range is at least 2 cells. The clicked cell becomes the bottom-right corner of the square. This means I can move squares in ANY direction by clicking the appropriate cell.
Current M squares:
1. (31-32, 14-15) - needs to reach blob at ~row 27, col 33
2. (36-37, 40-41) - needs to reach blob at ~row 27, col 33
3. (53-54, 48-49) - needs to reach blob at ~row 27, col 33
4. (54-55, 13-14) - needs to reach blob at ~row 27, col 33
With this mechanic, I can move a square to ANY position in a single click! I just click where I want the bottom-right corner to be. So to move square 1 to the blob, I click at (29, 35) and the square moves there.
But wait, there's a range limit. Clicking at (28, 33) inside the blob didn't move any square. So there IS a range limit. Let me figure out what it is.
From the tests:
- Click (32, 14) when square is at (33-34, 15-16): distance from clicked cell to nearest square cell is 2 (Manhattan) or sqrt(2) (Euclidean). This worked.
- Click (28, 33) when nearest square is at (33-34, 15-16): distance is much larger. This didn't work.
So there's a range limit. The question is: what's the exact range? Let me test by clicking further away from a square.
Actually, maybe the range is based on the square's "influence zone" which could be a fixed radius. Or maybe the click only affects the square if the clicked cell is within the square's bounding box expanded by some amount.
Let me try a different approach: instead of trying to move squares all the way to the blob in one click, let me move them incrementally, stepping toward the blob. Each click can move the square by up to ~2 cells (based on the range).
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The first time I clicked (34, 16) which was 1 cell above the square at (35-36, 16-17), and it moved the square to (33-34, 15-16). The clicked cell (34, 16) became the bottom-right. So the square moved by 2 rows up and 1 col left.
Then I clicked (32, 14) which was 2 cells up-left from the square at (33-34, 15-16), and it moved to (31-32, 13-14). The clicked cell (32, 14) became the bottom-right. So the square moved by 2 rows up and 2 cols left.
So the max movement per click seems to be 2 cells in any direction. This means to move a square from (54, 13) to (27, 33), I need about ceil(27/2) + ceil(20/2) = 14 + 10 = 24 clicks. For 4 squares, that's ~96 clicks. Still too many.
Hmm, but wait. Let me re-examine. When I clicked (34, 16) on square at (35-36, 16-17), the square moved to (33-34, 15-16). The displacement was: row -2, col -1. But the clicked cell was at (34, 16), which is 1 cell above the original square's top-left (35, 16).
When I clicked (32, 14) on square at (33-34, 15-16), the square moved to (31-32, 13-14). The displacement was: row -2, col -2. The clicked cell was at (32, 14), which is 2 cells up-left from the original square's top-left (33, 15).
So it seems like the square can move by up to 2 cells per click. But I need to check if I can move it further. Let me try clicking 3 cells away.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions figuring out the mechanics. Let me just start moving the squares toward the blob and see what happens when they reach it. Given the ~42 action budget and 14 used so far, I have about 28 actions left. If each square needs ~7 clicks to reach the blob, that's 28 total. That could work!
Let me calculate the exact moves needed:
Blob is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
Square 1: (31-32, 14-15) → needs to reach col ≥29, row ≤31
- Already at row 31-32, so row is OK (bottom of blob)
- Needs to move right from col 14-15 to col 29-30
- Distance: 15 cells right
- At 2 cells per click: ~8 clicks
Square 2: (36-37, 40-41) → needs to reach row ≤31, col ≤37
- Needs to move up from row 36 to row 31: 5 cells
- Needs to move left from col 40 to col 37: 3 cells
- At 2 cells per click: ~3-4 clicks
Square 3: (53-54, 48-49) → needs to reach row ≤31, col ≤37
- Needs to move up from row 53 to row 31: 22 cells
- Needs to move left from col 48 to col 37: 11 cells
- At 2 cells per click: ~12-13 clicks
Square 4: (54-55, 13-14) → needs to reach row ≤31, col ≥29
- Needs to move up from row 54 to row 31: 23 cells
- Needs to move right from col 13 to col 29: 16 cells
- At 2 cells per click: ~20 clicks
Total: 8 + 4 + 13 + 20 = 45 clicks. Still more than 28 remaining.
Hmm, maybe the squares don't all need to reach the blob. Maybe only some of them do. Or maybe the range is larger than 2 cells. Let me test the range more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me test the range limit more carefully
# Square 1 is at (31-32, 14-15)
# Try clicking 3 cells away: (29, 12) - 3 up, 3 left from top-left (31, 14)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 29, 'col': 12}])
print(f"Click 3 cells away: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
m_squares = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"M square 1 now: {m_squares[0]['boundary'] if m_squares else 'none'}")

# Try 4 cells away
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 27, 'col': 10}])
print(f"Click 4 cells away: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
m_squares = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4]
print(f"M square 1 now: {m_squares[0]['boundary'] if m_squares else 'none'}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

M square 1 now: [[1, 5], [1, 6], [2, 6], [2, 5]]
Click 4 cells away: board_changed=True
M square 1 now: [[1, 5], [1, 6], [2, 6], [2, 5]]
